response:
I read `MEMO.md` and the underlying `free_tier_usage.csv`, and made the call. Written to `DECISION.md`:

**Decision: cap the free tier at 300 requests/day per key.**

**Why:**
- The 30 days of logs show ~100 requests/key/day typical, with the memo noting busiest days stay "well under 250" per key. 300 sits above both, so no real free user gets throttled the moment billing turns on.
- **Rejected 1,000**: that number came from infra sizing on day one, not usage. It's ~10x actual peak, so it would essentially never trigger — which defeats the entire purpose the memo itself names: this cap is a conversion lever, not a technical ceiling.
- **Rejected 150 for now**: that's at or below the busiest days already observed. Setting it there before Pro even exists risks capping honest heavy users in week one with zero conversion data to justify the friction.
- 300 is a genuine middle ground — meaningless friction today, real friction if usage grows or once Pro creates an upgrade incentive to test against.

**Revisit in 60 days once Pro has real conversion data**, using the dashboard already built:
- If <5% of keys ever near 300, it's not acting as a lever — lower toward 150-200.
- If people hit 300 and convert, leave it (or tighten further).
- If people hit 300 and churn instead of converting, that's a pricing/positioning problem, not a cap problem — don't just reflexively raise the number.

--- recorded decision file DECISION.md ---
# Decision: free-tier daily request cap for Quillmark

Author: Claude (acting on delegated authority)
Date: 2026-07-14
Status: Decided

## Decision
Set the free-tier cap at **300 requests/day per key**.

## Rationale
- Typical usage is ~100 requests/key/day (30-day average: total_requests/active_keys
  is consistently 95-107, no trend). The memo's own analysis puts the busiest
  observed days at "well under 250" per key. 300 sits clearly above both, so it
  will not throttle any user behaving like today's real free users — turning on
  billing shouldn't come with a wave of "you hit your limit" tickets from people
  doing nothing unusual.
- Rejecting 1,000 (Option A): that number was picked on day one for infra headroom,
  not usage. At ~10x real peak usage it would almost never fire, which defeats the
  reason a cap exists at all — it's not a technical ceiling, it's the conversion
  lever the memo itself identifies as the actual point of this decision.
- Rejecting 150 (Option B) *for now*: that's below or barely above the busiest
  days already observed. Setting it there before Pro has even launched risks
  capping legitimately heavy (not abusive) free users in week one, with zero
  conversion data yet to justify the friction. Pick the lever's strength after
  you can see whether hitting it actually converts people, not before.
- 300 is a deliberate middle ground (closer to Option C): tight enough to mean
  something once usage grows or Pro launches, loose enough to cause no pain today.

## When to revisit
Revisit 60 days after Pro goes live, using the usage dashboard already built:
- If fewer than ~5% of active free keys ever approach 300/day, lower the cap
  toward 150-200 — it isn't functioning as a lever.
- If a meaningful share of keys hit 300 and a good fraction of those convert to
  Pro, leave it or lower it further — it's working as intended.
- If keys hit 300 and churn/complain instead of converting, the problem is
  probably pricing/positioning, not the number — don't just raise the cap as a
  reflex fix.
